conversation.message: I smoke bidis every day. My family says it's bad
Let's focus on how many bidis you smoke in a day. How many bidis do you typically smoke each day?
I smoke 15–20 bidis every day. Sometimes more when I'm stressed at work
I notice you often smoke more when you're stressed at work. Can you tell me more about what happens when you feel stressed at work that makes you want to smoke even more?
When I'm stressed at work, i get angry easily . smoking helps me calm for a little while
So you feel that smoking helps calm you down when you're stressed and angry at work. How does your anger feel in that moment, is it a sudden burst or a slow-building tension?
usually a slow building tension. i get angry and smoking helps with that
It sounds like the anger builds up slowly, and smoking seems to provide some relief for you. What do you think would happen if you didn't smoke when you felt that slow-building tension?
If I didn't smoke, the tension would probably keep building and I'd get more angry. It's hard to imagine handling it without the bidi

You're feeling stuck in imagining how you'd manage your tension without smoking. What's the first thing that comes to mind when you think of situations where you wouldn't be able to smoke, like if you were at a meeting or a deadline was approaching?
i have been smoking for 5 years and i want to quit
How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
around 2 packs
How often do you usually smoke a pack?
as i said i smoke 2 packs a day

You've confirmed that you smoke 2 packs a day. 

What type of cigarettes do you usually smoke, are they menthols or non-menthols?
menthols
You smoke menthols. Are you comfortable discussing how long you've been smoking?
since 5 years
You've mentioned smoking for 5 years. Are you currently looking to quit smoking?
yes
So you said yes to wanting to quit smoking. How long have you been thinking about quitting?
from past 2 - 3 months
You've been thinking about quitting for 2-3 months. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
i am been smoking cigaretes for 5 years and i want to quit. i have trid nicotine gums, but they have not worked.
You've been trying to quit for a few months now, and you've used nicotine gum before. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
i have been smoking for 5 years and i want to quit. i have tried nicotine gums but they did not help.
You've been trying to quit for a while now. How many cigarettes do you smoke per day?
clear
You've been smoking for 5 years and you've tried nicotine gums before. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
i have been smoking for 5 years and i want to quit. i have tried nicotine gums before but that did not help
You've been honest about your struggles with nicotine gum. What happens right before you reach for a cigarette?
Hi, I have been smoking for about 10 years now. My doctor told me I should try to quit.
You've been smoking for about 10 years now. What makes you think it's time to quit, considering you've been doing it for so long?
Honestly my chest has been hurting lately and my kids keep asking me to stop. I guess it just hit me that I need to do something about it.

It sounds like the pain in your chest and your kids' concerns have really brought things to a head for you, and you're feeling a strong motivation to make a change. Can you think of anything that usually happens around the same time as when you feel like smoking, like a certain time of day or a specific situation?
Yeah, I always smoke first thing in the morning with my coffee. And whenever I get stressed at work I step outside for a smoke. It is kind of how I cope I guess.

You've mentioned smoking with your coffee in the morning and during work stress. Would it be helpful to delay your morning smoke by 10 minutes and drink a glass of water instead, to see if that can help break the automatic habit?
I tried cutting down before but I always end up going back. I tried nicotine gum once but it didnt really help. I just dont know if I have the willpower.

It sounds like you're really struggling with willpower and that's making you doubt your ability to quit. Are you open to trying something different this time, like replacing smoking with a healthier morning routine?
Maybe. I just get really strong cravings, especially when I am stressed. What can I actually do when the craving hits really bad?

It sounds like stress is a major trigger for you, and strong cravings are a significant challenge. Have you tried taking a short walk or doing some light physical activity when you're feeling stressed or craving a cigarette?
i have been smoking for 10 years and i want to quit

So you've been smoking for 10 years and you want to quit - that's a big step already. What usually triggers you to reach for a cigarette, like when you're feeling a certain way or in a particular situation?
mostly stress i think, when it is stress ful at work. Also I smoke after eating food

You mentioned work stress and smoking after eating food are your triggers - do you think replacing your cigarette breaks with short walks could help you manage stress and break the habit of smoking after meals?
i dont know. i have tried it before but the craving is strong i will end up smoking.
You've tried quitting before, but the cravings were too strong - what do you think would happen if you delayed smoking after meals by just 10 minutes to see if the craving passes on its own?
maybe but after 10 mins , i end up smoking because of cravings. it fells like i can not work anymore becuase of headache

You mentioned that the craving is strong and you end up smoking after 10 minutes because of it - have you ever tried distracting yourself with something else during those 10 minutes, like taking a few deep breaths or grabbing a glass of water?

i have tried deep breathing, but when headache appears i cant focus on anything. i feel like i cant stop smoking. my wife keeps fighting me about it and it makes the stress worse and i will smoke more

Given that you've tried deep breathing but struggle to focus on it when you have a headache, I want to ask: Have you considered keeping your hands busy with something else while you're experiencing those headaches, like doodling or playing with a stress ball?
its not just keeping busy. i feel hopeless. my wife keeps fighting me. i feel like i cannot do this

You feel like you can't do this because your wife's fighting makes the stress worse, and you end up smoking more - have you thought about talking to her about finding a different way to support you in your quit attempt, rather than fighting it?
She will not listen. She just says stop smoking that's it
You feel like talking to your wife about this isn't an option right now - how do you think your life would change if you were able to quit smoking, without her support, but with your own motivation?
my health will improve and maybe my wife will be happy. my kids also do not like me smoking. but i need some real help this time

It sounds like you're feeling a mix of motivations to quit, including wanting to improve your health and make your family happy. Can you imagine how rewarding it would be to reach a milestone, like staying smoke-free for a day or a week, and celebrate that success with something you enjoy?
yes i want that. but i dont know where to start
You're feeling overwhelmed and not sure where to begin. Can you tell me, what's one small change you could make in your daily routine that would make quitting smoking feel a little more manageable?
maybe i can try not smoking after eating food. that is the easiest i can think of.
Let's focus on making that small change a reality. If you try not smoking after eating food, how would you plan to distract yourself from the urge to smoke during meals?
i dont know

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about how to deal with cravings during meals. What do you think might happen if you had a plan in place for handling those urges, like having a piece of gum or a healthy snack nearby?
i can try the gum
You mentioned trying the gum, can you see yourself using it in situations when you feel the urge to smoke after eating?
yes

You're feeling more open to trying the gum. Now that you've got a plan for using gum when you feel the urge to smoke after eating, how do you think you can apply this approach to the other situations where you feel stressed, like at work?
i dont know, i can try at those situations as well

Now that we've talked about using gum when you feel the urge to smoke after eating, can you imagine taking a short walk outside during your lunch break to get some fresh air and clear your head when you feel stressed at work?
that did not work for me
You mentioned taking a short walk outside didn't work for you at work. Can you help me understand why you think it didn't work, so we can build on it?
i am busy
I understand that you feel like you're too busy to take a short walk outside. Can we think about a way to break down that busy time, like taking a few minutes to take a short walk during your breaks?
no

You've expressed that taking a short walk outside didn't work for you and you feel too busy, and you're not open to that idea. Can you think of any other small activities that you might be able to do during your busy breaks at work to help manage your stress and cravings?
i can try the gum lets see how this goes
You're willing to try the gum, which is a great first step. How would you like to use the gum, such as when you feel a craving coming on or right after meals?
i can try that
You're open to trying the gum, which is a great first step. Would you be willing to try placing the gum between your cheek and gum to release the nicotine more slowly, as I suggested?
yes
Now that you've expressed willingness to try the gum, how would you like to remind yourself of the benefits of quitting smoking to boost your motivation when cravings strike?
i want to stop smoking beacuse of my health
Now that you're motivated to quit for your health, can you imagine a scenario where someone offers you a cigarette, and how you'd respond?
i will deny it

Now that you've thought about denying a cigarette, let's practice a response that might feel more authentic to you, like saying, "Thank you, but I'm not interested." How would you feel if you said this to someone who offered you a cigarette?
hello i am 45 years old. and i have been chewing tobacco for 5 years. i want to stop
You're 45 and have been chewing tobacco for 5 years, and now you're ready to stop. What are some situations or emotions that make you reach for the tobacco when you don't want to?
after lunch i always feel like taking it. and sometimes when i am stiing free and doing nothing

Given your history of reaching for tobacco when feeling free and doing nothing, I'm wondering if getting involved in a small activity, like taking a short walk, could help shift your focus away from the urge to chew tobacco after lunch.
i tried walking but i still feel the urge . it does not go away
You've tried walking, but the urge remains. Have you considered talking to someone, like me, about the urge, to see if that might help reduce its intensity?
I have been smoking bidi's for 8 years. Doctor said i should stop

You've been smoking bidi's for 8 years, and it's clear that the doctor's advice has motivated you to quit. Have you tried avoiding or substituting the post-lunch urge with something else, like a short walk or a healthier snack?
I have been smoking bidi's for 8 years. Doctor said i should stop

It sounds like you've been smoking bidis for a long time and your doctor is encouraging you to stop. What's been going through your mind lately about quitting, especially since your doctor mentioned it?
i feel like i should stop smoking becuase i am feeling tierd easily

It sounds like feeling tired easily is a big motivator for you to quit smoking. What's a typical day look like for you, and are there any specific situations or times when you tend to feel the most tired?
morning time. I wake up and first thing i do is smoke. also after eating

It sounds like smoking in the morning and after eating are two specific situations that seem to trigger your desire to smoke. Can you think of a way to break the routine of smoking right after waking up, like doing something small to delay reaching for a bidi?
i have been smoking cigarettes for 10 years. my doctor told me to quit.
It sounds like you've been smoking for a long time, and your doctor has advised you to quit. What's been going through your mind since you found out you need to quit?
i know i should stop but it is very hard . i have tried before also but i coudnt do it
I can sense your frustration and difficulty in quitting. What do you usually do when you feel like you can't quit, like you did before?
i should just give up and start smoking
It sounds like you're feeling hopeless about quitting and considering just giving up. What situations or emotions often make you want to reach for a cigarette when you're feeling this way?
Mostly stress. When work is too much I feel like I need a cigarette to calm down. Also when I am with friends who smoke
It sounds like stress is a big challenge for you, and it often leads you to want a cigarette. Have you ever tried taking a short walk when you feel stressed, just to see if it helps calm you down?
i tried walking but it does not help much
It sounds like walking hasn't helped you manage stress in the past. Are you open to trying something else to calm down when you feel stressed, like a different kind of activity or distraction?
yes

It sounds like you're open to trying something new to manage stress, and that's great progress. Would you be willing to try "urge surfing" - observing your craving without acting on it, and seeing how it fades over time?
what is urge surfing , i do not understand

Urge surfing is when you simply observe your craving for a cigarette without acting on it, almost like surfing a wave - you ride it out until it passes. It's a way to learn that cravings are temporary and will fade on their own. Can you imagine trying to surf your next craving, just observing it without giving in?
Okay I understand. But my cravings last very long. I don't know if I can wait it out
Can you tell me, what do you think would happen if you rode out a craving for just 3-5 minutes, without giving in to a cigarette, to see if it fades like you've heard it can?
Maybe I can try. 3-5 minutes sounds possible. I will try next time
Now that you're considering trying urge surfing, can you think of a specific situation this week where you might practice it, like when you're feeling stressed at work?
Yes when I am at work and feel stressed I will try it.
Now that you've identified a specific situation where you'll try urge surfing, how do you think you can use a stress ball or something to keep your hands busy while you ride out that craving?
stress ball sounds good
Now that you've settled on using a stress ball to keep your hands busy, have you thought about where you'll keep the stress ball at work, so it's easily accessible when you need it?
yes
Now that you know you'll have a stress ball at work, can you think of a small reward you can give yourself when you successfully ride out a craving using urge surfing?
I have been smoking for 10 years.
You've been smoking for 10 years, that's a long time. What triggers you to light up, is there anything in particular that makes you reach for a cigarette?
Work stress is my main trigger.

You've identified work stress as your main trigger, which can be really overwhelming. What's driving you to want to quit smoking now, is there something specific that's made you think about giving up?
I want to quit because my health is getting worse.
It sounds like your health concerns are really motivating you to quit. When you feel stressed at work, what's one thing you could say to yourself to remind you of why you're trying to quit?
Yes I will try urge surfing when I feel stressed at work.

You've already identified urge surfing as a strategy that works for you, but sometimes taking a short walk or doing some light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings. Would you be willing to try taking a short walk instead of smoking during your work breaks?
I have been smoking for 10 years and work stress is my trigger. I want to quit for my health.

You've identified work stress as your trigger, and you want to quit smoking for your health. How do you think your day would change if you took a short walk outside instead of smoking during your work breaks?
I have been smoking for 10 years and my doctor told me to quit

You've been smoking for 10 years, and now your doctor has advised you to quit - that's quite a challenge. 

What situations or emotions do you think make you want to reach for a cigarette?
Mostly when I am stressed. And after eating food also I feel like smoking

You mentioned feeling stressed and wanting to smoke after eating - those are two things we can work with. 

Do you think taking a short walk after meals might help you break the habit of smoking afterwards?
I tried walking but after food I just feel like something is missing. The urge does not go
You've tried walking after eating, but the urge to smoke still persists. Sometimes, changing your environment can help reduce cravings - would you be open to trying that?
What do you mean change environment? I eat at home only

It sounds like you're worried that changing your environment might not be possible since you usually eat at home. How about trying something small like moving to a different room in your house to eat, or even just taking a few steps outside to the patio or a balcony, to see if that makes a difference?
I am 38 years old. I have been smoking cigarettes for 15 years. I smoke about half a pack a day. I really want to quit now
You've been smoking about half a pack a day for 15 years, that's a long-standing habit. What does a typical day look like for you in terms of when you usually smoke?
Morning with tea is the first one. Then after lunch. And evening when I come back from work I smoke 2-3 cigarettes
I sense that you're taking the first step by sharing your daily smoking routine, which is a big part of quitting. What is it about quitting smoking that you feel is most important to you right now?
My wife is pregnant. I don't want to smoke around her or the baby. Also I am getting breathing problems

It sounds like you're motivated to quit for your wife and your own health. Would you be open to trying a technique where you delay smoking for a short period after a trigger, like after lunch or after work, to see if the urge passes?
i cn try but after lunch the urge is very strong. I don't know if I can wait
You're feeling uncertain about waiting after lunch when you experience a strong urge. Can you imagine taking a short walk after lunch instead of smoking, to see if it helps manage the craving?
i have tried walking before, it did not help much
You've tried walking before, but it didn't help much. Can you imagine trying a new approach, like taking a few deep breaths after lunch to see if it helps calm the craving?
hi, i am smoking for 10 years and i want to change my lifestyle now
You mentioned you're trying to change your lifestyle now, can you tell me more about what you mean by that?
I am smoking for 5 years and i want to quit
You want to quit smoking after 5 years of struggling with this habit. When you feel like smoking the most, is it when you're stressed, or is it in certain situations or with certain people?
I usally smoke in the morining when i wake up and i smoke when i get stressed at work
You've been honest about your smoking habits, especially how morning and work stress trigger you to smoke. What is it about quitting smoking that motivates you to want to make a change?
my children do not like me smoking and it has been affcting my health also

Since you've been motivated to quit for the sake of your children and your health, I'm wondering if you'd be open to replacing your morning smoking habit with a healthier routine, like taking a short walk after waking up?
i tried short walk but it does not help much
You've already tried a short walk and it didn't seem to help much. Are you open to considering other activities that you could replace smoking with, like chewing gum or eating a healthy snack?
i could try having chewing gum i never tried it

You're open to trying chewing gum - that's a great first step. How do you think you would like to use the gum, specifically, do you want to chew it right away in the morning or wait until you feel the urge to smoke?
i do not know suggest me
You're open to trying chewing gum, and that's a great step. Would you like me to help you figure out a plan for how to use the gum, like setting a specific time or situation to use it?
yes

You seem open to trying chewing gum and willing to set a plan for its use. How would you like to choose a specific time or situation, like the morning or during work stress, to use the gum as a replacement for smoking?
i could do that
It feels like you're moving forward with confidence, and I wish you all the best as you begin this new chapter.
i have been smoking for 10 years and i want to quit
You've been smoking for 10 years and have a strong desire to quit. What do you think triggered the urge to smoke today, was it a particular situation or feeling?
i mean i usually smoke in the morning and when i get stressed at work

I can see that stress at work is a big trigger for you, and morning routines are also a habit for you. How does the idea of quitting smoking make you feel, is it a source of anxiety or a sense of hope and freedom for you?
i mean i want to quit smoking becuase my kids dont like it. also i am gaining wight

You're motivated by your kids and also concerned about your health. Given your family is a big motivator for you, could you imagine rewarding yourself with something special for each milestone you reach, like a week without smoking, to celebrate your progress and show your kids the effort you're putting in?
i have never tried it but i can try and see that

I'm glad you're open to trying new ideas. Now that we've discussed rewarding yourself for milestones, how do you think you could apply this idea to the stressful situations at work that trigger your smoking cravings?
i dont know

You've expressed uncertainty about how to apply the idea of rewarding yourself to stressful situations at work. What would happen if, the next time you feel stressed and reach for a cigarette, you instead took a few deep breaths and reminded yourself of a reward you could give yourself when you stay smoke-free?
i can try but how?

Now that you're willing to try a new approach, let's break it down. How about we start small and identify one specific stressful work situation where you often crave a cigarette, so we can brainstorm some ideas for coping with it?
when i get stressed at work i just go out and take a smoke break

It sounds like taking a smoke break is a big part of your routine when you're stressed at work. What would happen if, instead of grabbing a cigarette, you took a short walk outside and got some fresh air?
i can try that
It sounds like you're feeling more open to exploring new stress-reducing habits, and I'm wishing you all the best on your journey.
i have tried the short walk outside. it did not help much
I'm sorry to hear that the short walk outside didn't help much. Can you think of anything else you enjoy doing that might give you a similar break from the stress that makes you want to smoke?
i do not know
Let's try a small, manageable goal together. If you were able to take a short walk outside, what if we started with a simple activity like taking three deep breaths instead, when you feel stressed?
i also tried that did not help

I understand you've tried taking three deep breaths and it didn't help. Have you considered trying a different way to distract yourself from the craving, such as engaging in a hobby or activity that you enjoy?
dude i will be in work

Given your work schedule tomorrow, let's focus on building some coping skills that can be applied in brief moments. Would you be open to trying the "urge surfing" technique, where you observe the craving without acting on it, and see if it helps you ride it out?
why did you not suggesting nicotine gum
You're wondering why I didn't suggest nicotine gum earlier. Can using nicotine gum as a way to manage cravings when you feel stressed tomorrow at work be something you'd like to try?
i have been smoking for 10 years
You've been smoking for 10 years, that's a long time. What's been going through your mind lately that makes you want to reach for a cigarette?
my wife is pregnant and i want to quit becuase of that

You're motivated to quit smoking because of your wife's pregnancy - that's a really strong reason. Can you tell me, what's been going through your mind when you think about quitting, and what feels challenging about giving up cigarettes right now?
i kind of feel light headed and when i dont smoke

You're feeling lightheaded when you don't smoke, which makes quitting even harder. Can you think of any activities that you've enjoyed in the past that might help keep your hands busy and distract you from these feelings?
not really
You said you're not really feeling motivated to quit. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about quitting for your baby's sake?
yes

You mentioned you feel lightheaded when you don't smoke, which makes quitting even harder. Do you think it might be helpful to try a technique called "urge surfing" to help you ride out these feelings without smoking?
Hi, I've been smoking cigarettes for about 2 years now.

I see you've been smoking for about 2 years now. 
What were some of the situations or experiences that led you to start smoking in the first place?
It was just kinda a thing that my friends did at parties and stuff, I guess. I didn't really think much of it, just joined in because everyone else was doing it.
It sounds like you started smoking in social situations, like parties. What do you usually do when you're hanging out with friends now that you've been smoking for a while?
We usually smoke when we're out at the campus quad, just chillin' and talking. Sometimes if we're studying for exams, we'll smoke to take a break.
It sounds like smoking has become a big part of your social routine, especially when hanging out with friends. What do you think you'd miss most about smoking when you're trying to quit?
I guess I'd miss the bonding time with my friends, you know, just relaxing together. It feels like a weird part of our group dynamic, and I don't want to be the one to ruin it by not smoking.

It sounds like you're worried about losing that connection with your friends when you quit smoking. Have you thought about having a conversation with your friends about your decision to quit, and maybe even getting their support in this process?
I don't know, that might make things kinda awkward. What if they don't take it seriously or they pressure me to keep smoking with them?

Considering your concerns about having an awkward conversation with your friends, I'd like to gently suggest having a conversation with them as soon as possible, before you start quitting. Can you imagine what you'd feel more comfortable saying to them, and how you'd like them to respond in support of your decision to quit?

I guess I'd say something like, "Hey guys, I've been thinking about cutting back on smoking, and I was wondering if you could support me with that." And I'd want them to be cool with it, you know, not make me feel like I'm missing out or anything.

That sounds like a great first step, acknowledging your desire for their support in quitting smoking. Now, what do you think would happen if one of your friends offered you a cigarette while you're trying to quit, and how would you politely decline?
I'd probably just say something like, "Nah, I'm trying to quit, thanks" or "I'm in the middle of stopping for a bit, let's find something else to do." I don't know, it's not that hard, right?
Now that you've thought about how you'd decline an offer, can you imagine a situation where your friends might actually pressure you to smoke with them, and how you'd handle that?

Like, if we're out at a party and they're all smoking and I'm the only one not, they might try to make me feel left out or something. I'd probably just stand my ground and say no again, or maybe even suggest doing something else, like getting some food or playing a game.

Based on your recent conversation about handling social situations where others are smoking, I'd like to gently suggest having one person in your life who knows you're trying to quit and can offer support when you need it. Can you think of someone who might be a good person for you to reach out to for support during this process?
My sister might be okay, she's always been pretty supportive and stuff. I don't know, we don't really talk about this kind of stuff though.

That's a good start with your sister, considering her as a potential source of support. Are you open to having an open and honest conversation with your sister about your struggles with quitting smoking and asking for her support in this process?

Yeah, I guess I could talk to her about it. It's just...I don't know, my parents know I smoke and they're not really involved or anything, and I'd rather my sister not tell them, if you know what I mean.

It sounds like you're concerned about sharing your struggles with your sister, and you're worried about your parents finding out. Can you imagine how your sister might respond if you were to have an open and honest conversation with her about your struggles with quitting smoking?
I guess she might be understanding and stuff, but then again, she might feel like she's stuck in the middle if I ask her not to tell our parents. I don't know, it's hard to say for sure.
Based on your sister's potential willingness to offer support, would you feel more confident in your ability to avoid smoking if she were aware of your struggles and could offer encouragement?
Maybe, I mean, just knowing that she's got my back and stuff could be helpful, yeah.

Since you're open to having an honest conversation with your sister, I was thinking that it might be helpful to write down the things you're worried about, like your parents finding out, and talk them through with her. Would you be willing to try that?
I've been smoking for almost 20 years. My doctor said my lungs are getting worse.
I can sense how concerned you are about your lung health. How often do you smoke when you're feeling stressed or anxious?
Actually, it's more like after meals, like right after dinner. My husband always teases me about how I always grab a cigarette then.

It sounds like after-dinner smoking is a big challenge for you, and your husband's teasing might be making it even harder. Are you open to trying something simple to break the connection between finishing dinner and reaching for a cigarette, like brushing your teeth immediately after dinner?
Brushing my teeth, that's a good idea I guess. But what if I forget? Or if I really want a cigarette?

That's a valid concern - it's normal for new habits to feel hard to stick to. Can you think of a way to remind yourself to brush your teeth right after dinner, like putting a sticky note on the fridge or setting an alarm on your phone?
I could leave a note on the fridge. But what if my kids see it and start asking questions?

That's a valid concern about your kids seeing the note - would you consider having a simple conversation with them ahead of time, explaining that you're trying to quit smoking for your health, and that the note is just a reminder to help you stay on track?
I don't know, it sounds like a lot to explain to them. They're still young, maybe I should just wait until I'm quit already.

It sounds like you're worried about explaining your quit attempt to your kids, and that's understandable. Can you think of a way to have a simple conversation with them that makes it easier for you, like telling them a simple truth like "Mom/Dad is trying to be healthier"?
That sounds like a good idea, something simple like that. But what if they ask why I need to be healthier?

That's a valid concern about your kids asking why you need to be healthier. Would you consider using that opportunity to explain the health benefits of quitting smoking in a way that's easy for them to understand, like "quitting smoking will help me feel stronger and be around for longer"?
That makes sense. I could say something like that. But I'm worried it won't make a difference if I've failed before.

That's a valid concern about past failures - many people who quit smoking have tried several times before succeeding. Have you noticed anything that was different about those times when you were able to resist the urge to smoke for a longer period, even if it was just for a day or two?
I remember when I was pregnant with my second child, I didn't smoke at all for 8 months. But after I had the baby, I just didn't think about it as much anymore, you know?
Since you didn't smoke for 8 months during your pregnancy, I'm curious, was it the pregnancy itself or being a mother at that time that made you less interested in smoking?
I think it was being a mother, especially knowing that I had a baby inside me, it just made me want to be healthier for them.

You've realized that being a mother, especially with a baby inside you, motivates you to be healthier. Can you imagine carrying that same sense of responsibility and motivation with you every day, even when you're not pregnant?
Yeah, I guess I can. It feels weird to say it out loud, but it's like having my kids rely on me to be healthy is the real reason I want to quit.

Since you've mentioned that the sense of responsibility for your kids motivates you to be healthier, I'm curious, how might you remind yourself of that feeling every day, especially on days when cravings strike, to help you stay on track with your quit plan?
I could, um, carry a picture of them with me, or look at one on my phone, that way I'll see their faces and remember why I'm doing this.

You're thinking of carrying a picture as a reminder of your kids and why you want to quit. Would it feel more meaningful to you to share with someone close to you that you're planning to carry this picture as a reminder, and ask them to hold you accountable?
I think I could ask my husband to remind me to look at the picture when I'm feeling stressed. He's always on my case about smoking anyway, so it wouldn't be too hard to ask him for help.

You're considering asking your husband to hold you accountable by reminding you to look at the picture when you're feeling stressed, and that's a great step in getting support. Would you feel more comfortable if he also knew the specific moments or situations where you usually feel the urge to smoke?
I smoke bidis, about 15 to 20 a day. My son keeps asking me to stop.

I can sense how much your son's request means to you, and it's clear that it's a big motivator for you to quit. What's been the hardest part for you so far in considering quitting bidis for your son's sake?

I guess what's missing is a way to calm down in those situations that doesn't involve smoking. I feel like I'm stuck in this loop – the social situation makes me anxious, I need something to calm down, so I smoke, and then it's harder to deal with the anxiety the next time around.

You mentioned feeling stuck in a loop when you're in social situations and need something to calm down. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside during these events to clear your head and calm down?

Yeah, I've taken short walks before, but it doesn't really help that much. I feel like I'm just delaying the anxiety, and it's still there when I go back to the party or whatever. Plus, it's hard to do that when you're in a group and people are expecting you to be there, you know?

Given your concerns about feeling delayed in coping with anxiety and not wanting to leave social situations, do you think it would be helpful to practice a calming technique while still in the social situation, rather than leaving it?

I don't know... I've tried meditation and stuff, but it's hard to focus in the middle of a party. I feel like I'm going to look weird or something. But at the same time, it's better than smoking, I guess.

You've tried meditation before, but it's hard to focus in social situations. Have you considered taking a few deep breaths in those moments, just to calm down and see if that makes a difference for you?

It sounds so simple, but yeah, maybe it could help. I mean, I guess taking a few deep breaths wouldn't make me look weird or anything like meditating would. What if it doesn't work, though? What if I still feel anxious?

That's a valid concern about what if it doesn't work, but let's focus on the possibility that it might help. Are you willing to give taking a few deep breaths in social situations a try for the next few days and see how it goes?
Yeah, okay... I'll try it. Just a few deep breaths, not like a big ordeal or anything. But if I'm still feeling anxious after that, can I still smoke?
You've taken a great step towards exploring some new coping strategies – wish you all the best as you move forward.
I feel like I've been trying to quit for so long, but this feels different. I'll try to remember to take those deep breaths.
It's wonderful to see you feeling hopeful and empowered – may this new sense of confidence carry you forward.
I think I might finally have a chance at breaking this cycle.

Now that we've explored a few deep breaths as a coping strategy, let's think about making a daily plan for managing cravings. Are you open to writing down when and where you experience cravings, and what you do to manage them, so we can start identifying patterns?
That sounds like a good idea, yeah. I can write that down. Do I have to track every time I feel a craving, though? That feels like a lot.
Not necessarily, we can start small – just tracking the times when you feel a strong craving, and what happens right before it.
My dad died of lung cancer last year. I've been smoking a pack a day for 18 years.

I'm so sorry to hear about your dad's passing. That must have been a really tough time for you. What's going through your mind when you think about why you've continued to smoke for so long despite the risk it poses to your health?
It's just a habit. It's always been there, you know? I mean, I know I should stop, but how am I supposed to just quit cold turkey?

It sounds like smoking has just become a part of your daily routine, something you're used to doing without even thinking about it. What do you typically do in a day that you associate with having a cigarette?
Breaks at work, when I'm stressed, and sometimes after dinner. It's just something I do when I'm taking a break and thinking about my day.

It sounds like breaks at work, stress, and after dinner are times when you associate with having a cigarette. I think it might be helpful to try and find a way to replace those cigarettes with something else, like a short walk, during those times. Would you be open to trying that?
I've tried that before, walking, but it's not the same. I don't know, I need something to calm my nerves when I'm stressed. Maybe it's just me, but I need a bit more than just walking.
You've mentioned needing something to calm your nerves when you're stressed. Have you ever tried drawing or doodling to help calm down, maybe we could explore that as an alternative to smoking?
I used to draw when I was a kid, but I haven't done it in years. I don't know if it would help, but I suppose it's worth a try. What's the harm, right?

You've had a positive reaction to the idea of drawing as a way to calm down, which is a great start. Now, how do you think you could incorporate drawing into your daily routine, especially during those times you mentioned earlier like breaks at work and after dinner?

I guess I could carry a small sketchbook with me and draw during my breaks at work. And at home, I could set aside a few minutes each night to draw before dinner. It's not a big commitment, just a few minutes a day.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about incorporating drawing into your daily routine. Now, how will you reward yourself when you successfully stick to drawing during those challenging times, like breaks at work and after dinner?

I think I'd like to set a small reward for myself, like going out for a cup of coffee or something, after a week of doing it consistently. That way, I'll have something to look forward to, something to motivate me to keep going.
Now that you've set a reward for yourself, how will you remind yourself of that reward and the progress you've made so far when those cravings or stressful moments come up?

I can put a small note on my desk at work or on my fridge at home, something to remind me of my goal and the reward waiting for me. And maybe I can also keep a small calendar to track my progress. Seeing it in black and white will help me stay on track.

You're feeling more organized and motivated by tracking your progress and reminding yourself of your goals. Now, let's talk about what happens when you inevitably encounter setbacks - how will you handle those situations when you slip up or have a craving?

I'll just go back to my routine, start fresh the next day, and not be too hard on myself. One slip-up doesn't mean I've failed completely, does it? I can just pick up where I left off, try not to think about it too much, and move forward.

You've mentioned picking up where you left off when you slip up, which is a good approach. How do you think you can use that same mindset to view a single cigarette as a slip-up, rather than a complete failure, and still move forward in your quitting journey?

I can think of it as a minor setback, not as a major defeat. One cigarette won't undo all my hard work so far. I can just take a deep breath, remind myself why I wanted to quit in the first place, and get back on track.

You're feeling more confident in handling setbacks by viewing them as minor setbacks rather than complete failures. How about using a similar mindset to prepare yourself for a high-risk situation, like a break at work or after dinner, when you're more likely to encounter cravings?

I can prepare myself ahead of time by reminding myself of my goals and triggers, and come up with a plan for what I'll do when I feel that urge to smoke. Like, if someone at work tries to tempt me with a cigarette during a break, I'll know exactly what to say and do.

You're making great progress in thinking ahead and preparing for high-risk situations. Now, let's think about what specific actions you can take during those moments to replace the habit of smoking, what could be something new to do instead of reaching for a cigarette?

I think I can use a few different things to help distract me and replace the habit. I can start with deep breathing exercises, or maybe listen to some calming music on my phone. I also have a small stress ball that I can squeeze when I feel anxious.

You've come up with some creative ideas to replace smoking with healthier habits. What do you think about incorporating short, gentle movements, like stretching or a few yoga poses, into your stress-reducing toolkit?
My boyfriend asked me to quit smoking. We're getting serious and I want to try.
It sounds like you're motivated by your boyfriend and the seriousness of your relationship. How long have you been smoking?
About 2 years now. I only started because all my coworkers smoke during breaks and I didn't want to be left out. It just became a habit, I guess.
So you started smoking to fit in with your coworkers, and it just became a habit. What kind of situations or activities typically make you want to smoke now?
It's usually during break times at work, when everyone's smoking outside. I feel left out if I don't join, and I also like the social part of it, chatting with my coworkers.

It sounds like you feel left out when your coworkers are smoking outside during breaks and you're not joining them. Would it be helpful to have someone from work, like a coworker or supervisor, know about your goal to quit smoking and offer support?
I don't know...I don't want people to think I'm weird or that I'm quitting for the wrong reasons. What if they start giving me weird looks or make it awkward for me during breaks?

It sounds like you're worried about people thinking you're weird or judging you for quitting. Can you imagine how you might politely say no to a cigarette in a situation like a coworker offering you one during a break?
I guess I could just say something like, "No thanks, I'm quitting" or "I'm trying to cut back." But what if they keep offering or asking me why?

You're thinking of ways to respond to coworkers who might offer you a cigarette. Would you be open to practicing a simple phrase to say in those situations, like "No thanks, I'm quitting," and repeating it a few times to feel more confident?
I suppose that couldn't hurt. Just saying the words out loud might make it feel more real, like I'm actually doing something.

Now that you're feeling more confident about saying no to a cigarette, let's try something else. How about we practice delaying your response by 10 minutes the next time you're offered a cigarette, just to see if the urge really is temporary?
That's a weird idea...I don't know if I can just delay it like that. I always feel like I need to act on it right away. But I guess it couldn't hurt to try, right?
You're unsure about delaying your response to a cigarette, but you're open to trying. Can you imagine what might happen to your need for a cigarette if you were to delay acting on it for 10 minutes?
I guess I might still want the cigarette after 10 minutes, but...but what if I don't? What if the craving just goes away? That's a pretty good reason to try, I suppose.

You're thinking that the craving might just go away if you delay acting on it for 10 minutes, that's a pretty empowering idea. Can you imagine using that 10-minute delay to distract yourself with a different activity, like taking a short walk or chatting with a coworker?
Yeah, I could do that. Talking to someone or just stretching my legs for a bit might make the 10 minutes go by faster, and if I'm really not thinking about smoking anymore, then it's not a big deal.
Now that you're feeling more open to trying the 10-minute delay, let's think about what might happen if someone offers you a cigarette in a social situation.
They'll just laugh and say I'm no fun, or something like that. But my boyfriend will be proud of me if I can do it...that's a pretty good motivator, I think.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about how others will react if you don't smoke in social situations, but you also see your boyfriend's pride as a motivator. Can you imagine celebrating your own progress and small victories along the way, like staying smoke-free at a party or gathering?
I could write it in a journal or something...just to keep track of how long I've gone without smoking. And maybe my boyfriend can help me celebrate, like we can go out for a nice dinner or something.

Now that you're thinking about celebrating your progress, would you be open to writing down your reasons for quitting smoking, so you can refer to them when you need a reminder of why you're doing this?
Yeah, that's a good idea. I can write down the reasons why I want to quit, like my boyfriend and feeling better about myself. What if I write them down and put them somewhere I'll see them every day?

You're thinking of putting your reasons for quitting in a place where you'll see them every day - that could be very helpful in staying motivated. Would you consider putting them somewhere you'll see them first thing in the morning, like on your fridge or bathroom mirror?
My doctor was very direct. He said if I don't quit, my heart is at serious risk. I'm 65.
It sounds like that was a tough conversation with your doctor. How many cigarettes do you smoke per day?
Two packs. It's always two packs.
It sounds like two packs is a daily habit for you. What time of day do you usually smoke your first cigarette?
First thing in the morning, as soon as I wake up.
You've mentioned that you smoke right after waking up. Can you think of something you could do immediately after waking up, instead of reaching for a cigarette, to start your day in a different way?
Drink some water, maybe. I drink a lot of coffee. Maybe I could drink water first.
You've mentioned drinking water as a possible alternative to your morning cigarette. What do you think about making drinking water your first morning habit, rather than coffee?
That's not so easy. Coffee's what wakes me up. Water's okay, but it's not the same.

You feel that coffee is a big part of your morning routine and that water just doesn't give you the same energy boost. Can you imagine what might happen if you still had your coffee, but started with a small glass of water before it, to see if that makes a difference?
I suppose it's worth trying. But it's not going to make me stop smoking entirely, is it? You're not saying that, are you?

You're right, we're not promising that one simple change will completely stop your smoking, but it's a step in the right direction. Are you willing to try this small experiment with the water before coffee and see how it affects your morning routine?
Yeah, I'll try. What if it doesn't make a difference?
You're feeling a bit uncertain, but taking this small step is a courageous first step towards creating positive change in your life.
My wife will be happy if I try. She's been on my case about this for years.
It sounds like your wife's support is a big motivator for you. Have you talked to her about how you're feeling about quitting, and what kind of support she might be able to offer you?
Yeah, I've talked to her. She's always telling me to quit, but I guess I needed her to see my doctor's warning to really get through to me.

Since you've talked to your wife and she's now more supportive, who in your life besides your wife could be a go-to person for you when you're feeling the urge to smoke, or when you need encouragement and support?
My son. He's a doctor too. He's always been against me smoking. I think he might be more understanding than my wife, actually.

It sounds like having your son's support could be really helpful. Would you be comfortable reaching out to him when you're feeling the urge to smoke, or would you prefer to let him know in a less intense moment, like after you've quit for a bit?
I'd rather talk to him later. When it's really over, you know?

Based on your concern about talking to your son later, I'm wondering if you think it might be helpful to have a plan in place for how you'll reach out to him for support when you're feeling the urge to smoke.
I guess that makes sense. I can call him after I've gone a week without smoking, or something. Then he can know I'm serious about quitting for good.
You're taking a thoughtful approach to reaching out for support – I wish you all the best on your path forward.
I play club cricket but I smoke secretly. My stamina is getting really bad.
So you're worried about your stamina in cricket, and you're smoking secretly. How long have you been smoking?
Not long, to be honest. A couple of years maybe. And it's only after practice now, I've never really been a social smoker.
So you've been smoking for about two years and it's mainly after cricket practice, not in social situations or at other times. What happens right after practice that makes you want to smoke?

The stress of playing, and also being alone after practice. It's not like I want to be by myself, but everyone else goes off somewhere and I just sit there, feel like I'm just trying to catch my breath.

You feel lonely and stressed after practice, which makes you want to smoke. Have you ever considered doing something to keep your hands busy right after practice, like drawing or doodling, to see if that might help distract you from the urge to smoke?

I don't know... it feels like a weird thing to do just sitting there drawing. But I guess it's worth a shot, I mean I've tried other things to distract myself like watching videos on my phone but that just makes me feel more tired.

You're not sure if drawing feels like a weird thing to do, but you're open to trying something new. Would you be willing to give drawing a try for just one practice session to see if it helps you feel a little more engaged and less inclined to smoke?
I suppose I could try it. But what if I'm really bad at it? I don't want to be walking around with some stupid drawing, that's embarrassing.
You're worried about being embarrassed, but what if you didn't show anyone your drawing and just used it as a way to keep your hands busy during practice?
I guess that would make it easier. But what if I still really want to smoke afterwards? Will that mean the drawing didn't work?

You're concerned that trying drawing won't work if you still feel like smoking afterwards, that's a valid worry. Would you be okay with focusing on just the experience of drawing during practice, without worrying about whether it's "working" to stop your cravings?
It's just that I feel like I'm going to have to quit smoking eventually, and I don't want to be doing something that's just a distraction if I'm not really committed to quitting.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about whether trying drawing is worth it if you're still not fully committed to quitting smoking. Can you think of anything that might help you feel more committed to quitting, even if it's just for a little while?
I guess the state team, I really want to make it next year. If I could quit smoking I might actually have a chance.

You think that quitting smoking might actually give you a chance to make it to the state team next year. Can you imagine what your life would be like if you were able to stay smoke-free for a whole month, and how that might impact your training and overall performance?
Like, I'd be able to run longer without getting tired, my breathing would be better... I feel like I'd be able to give it my all on the field, instead of holding back because I'm so winded.

Now that you've thought about how quitting smoking might improve your performance and overall well-being, I'd like to suggest a technique to help you cope with loneliness, which is one of your biggest triggers. Would you be open to practicing deep breathing exercises when you feel isolated or alone?
That sounds like it could help. I've had some coaches teach us breathing exercises to calm down in heated moments, but I never thought to use it for feeling alone.

That's a great connection to make between breathing exercises and feeling calm in sports moments. Now, would you be willing to start practicing deep breathing exercises for a short period, like 5 minutes, to see how it affects your feelings of loneliness?
Yeah, I'll try it. But can I do it right after practice, when the worst of the loneliness feeling is usually there? Maybe it'll help me get through the worst of it.
I'm so glad you feel empowered to use this new tool in a moment when you need it most.
I think I'm ready to try, yeah. Just... don't expect me to get all zen and meditate or anything, okay?
Now that you're willing to try deep breathing exercises, what specific situation or trigger will you use as a starting point to practice this technique?
I work from home and my smoking has gone way up. About 12 a day now.

That can be isolating and tempting to turn to smoking when you're at home all day. What do you think is driving you to smoke so much while working from home, is it the lack of structure or something else?
It's probably a mix. Cabin fever, I guess. Sometimes I just need a break from the same four walls.

It sounds like you're feeling trapped at home and turning to smoking as a way to escape that feeling. Have you ever tried doing something different, like taking a short walk outside, when you're feeling overwhelmed and need a break?
I usually just smoke instead of going for a walk. It's quicker, and I don't have to deal with the kids asking for things while I'm away.

I think it's interesting that you mention smoking is quicker than taking a walk, which suggests that you're looking for a quick escape from your feelings of cabin fever. Would you be open to trying a different way to get a break that doesn't involve smoking?
I don't know...it's hard to think of anything else that's as quick as smoking. Taking a walk would take like 10 minutes just to get ready, you know?

I understand that taking a walk might seem like a long process compared to smoking. How about we try a shorter activity, like taking a few deep breaths or doing some quick stretching, as a way to pause and break up your day when you're feeling overwhelmed?
That sounds kind of silly, like it wouldn't make a difference. But I guess it's worth a try. What kind of stretches, though?

You think deep breathing and stretching might seem silly, but you're willing to try it. How open are you to using a stress ball or a fidget toy to keep your hands busy when you feel the urge to smoke?
I don't know...it sounds a bit childish, but I guess it could be worth a shot. What's the worst that could happen, right?
Let's try using a stress ball or a fidget toy as a way to keep your hands busy when you feel the urge to smoke. Would you like to bring one to our next session to try it out?
Yeah, okay. I can try that. But what about the kids being at home, I don't want them to see me playing with a fidget toy or think it's weird.

You're worried about what your kids might think if they see you with a fidget toy. How would you feel if you kept the fidget toy hidden in a private place, like your pocket or desk drawer, when you're at home?
I guess that would be okay. I just don't want them to see me using it, if that makes sense. I don't know, though, it still feels a bit weird to me.

You're feeling a bit hesitant about using a fidget toy, and it's natural to worry about what others might think. What's the main reason you're still unsure about trying this tool to help manage your cravings?

I think it's just because...I don't know, it feels like a Band-Aid solution or something. Like, I'm still dealing with the root problem, which is my smoking habit, and it feels like the fidget toy is just a quick fix.

You're worried that the fidget toy might just be a quick fix, and you're still dealing with the underlying issue of your smoking habit. Are you open to exploring ways to address the root problem, rather than just managing your cravings with a tool?

I guess so. I mean, I know I need to address the habit itself. It's just hard because...I feel like I'm doing okay, you know? I don't wake up in the morning thinking "Oh, I have to quit smoking." It's more like "I just do it, and that's it."

You're saying it's hard to see the habit as a problem when it feels so automatic. Would you be open to exploring ways to help make quitting feel less like a huge change, and more like a series of small, manageable steps?
Yeah, that might be doable. Like, instead of quitting cold turkey, could I try to reduce my smoking, little by little? Maybe cut back to 10 cigarettes a day, or something like that.

Now that you're considering reducing your smoking, a little at a time, would it be helpful to start tracking your daily smoking habits, so we can get a better sense of what's working and what areas we could improve on?
I don't know...I tried tracking my smoking before, and it just made me feel worse. Seeing how much I was smoking every day was really depressing.

I understand you're hesitant to try tracking again because it made you feel worse last time. Would you be open to trying a different approach, like setting small rewards for yourself when you reach certain milestones, like staying smoke-free for a day or a week?
My divorce came through 6 months ago and I went from 5 cigarettes to 20 a day.
I can imagine how tough 6 months have been for you, especially with the divorce. What was going through your mind when you went from 5 cigarettes to 20 a day, do you think?
To be honest, I didn't really notice it happening. I thought it was just stress relief. But now it's like, I'm smoking all the time.

It sounds like you've been feeling overwhelmed by your smoking habit lately, and it's like it's taken over. What if we tried to find some other way to cope with stress, like keeping your hands busy with a stress ball or a fidget toy?
I don't know, I don't think that's going to fill the void, you know? Smokers always say "just quit cold turkey" but it's not that easy when you're alone at night and the stress is just sitting there.

It sounds like you're worried that just removing cigarettes won't fill the void, that there's a deeper emotional need that's driving your smoking. How would you feel about trying to identify what that void is, and exploring ways to fill it that don't involve smoking?

I guess I just feel scared of looking at it. It's easier to just keep telling myself it's just a bad habit. But at the same time... I do want to cut back, I don't want to be this guy who's always smoking.

I understand you're afraid of looking at the deeper emotional need behind your smoking, but you also want to cut back. Would it help if we started by identifying a small, non-smoking related activity you can replace your smoking habit with, like chewing gum or taking a short walk at night when you feel stressed?
I mean, gum's okay, but it's not going to last me until the pack runs out. And walking sounds nice, but it gets dark so early now, I'm not sure I'll be able to do it.

With your concerns about gum not lasting long and walking not being feasible due to darkness, what if we tried gradually increasing your walking distance or time over the next few weeks, so it becomes a more manageable habit?
I don't know... I've tried setting goals for myself before and they just don't stick. I'll start strong, and then it's like I'll forget what I'm doing or something.

You've tried setting goals before and found it didn't work, which might be why you're skeptical about making a daily plan for managing cravings. Would you be open to exploring a more flexible approach, where you focus on one day at a time, rather than setting long-term goals?
I guess so. But even just one day at a time sounds like a lot of work.

Now that you're open to a more flexible approach, how do you think it would be to break down the idea of managing cravings into smaller, manageable steps, rather than overwhelming yourself with a whole day at a time?
I don't know, it sounds like a lot of mental math. But if it means I don't have to think about quitting for, like, an hour, that's a start, I guess.

It sounds like you're willing to take small steps, which is a great starting point. What specific things do you think you could realistically do in a single hour that would help you manage cravings and move forward with quitting?
I mean, I think I could just not have a pack of cigarettes in the house, that way I wouldn't have them staring at me.

That's a great start, not having cigarettes at home can definitely help reduce temptation. Can you imagine what it would be like to have someone to talk to when you're feeling overwhelmed and craving a cigarette?
.. my sister used to be really supportive before everything fell apart. I don't know, maybe it's time I called her again.
That's a great idea about reaching out to your sister - she could be a valuable support system for you. Are you feeling ready to call her and talk to her about quitting cigarettes?
.. I don't know if I'm ready for that conversation, but I think I need to try. Yeah, I'll call her.

With you feeling open to reaching out to your sister, I'd like to suggest that you consider talking to her about your cravings and stress in the moment, could you imagine how helpful it might be to have her listening and offering support when you're feeling overwhelmed?
Yeah... yeah, that sounds really good. I can already feel a weight lifting off my shoulders just talking about it.

I can already feel a weight lifting off your shoulders just talking about it. How would you feel about setting up a specific time to call your sister and talk to her about quitting cigarettes today or tomorrow?
I'm spending 2000 rupees a month on cigarettes and money is very tight for my family.

I can sense how stressful it must be for you to spend that much on cigarettes when every rupee counts for your family. How do you usually feel when you're at work or in situations that make you reach for a smoke break?
To be honest, I just smoke because it helps me calm down during those stressful moments when there's an argument with my wife or when I'm dealing with a difficult customer at work.

Given that we're in the planning phase and need to introduce a relevant coping strategy, I'll suggest a technique that might help you manage stress without smoking. Have you ever taken a few deep breaths when you're feeling stressed, and if so, how did that make you feel?
Deep breathing, yeah, I remember my yoga instructor telling me about it, but to be honest, I never stuck with it long enough to see if it really works.

Given that you have some experience with deep breathing exercises from yoga, can you imagine incorporating it into your daily routine, especially during stressful moments, and what might make it more sustainable for you this time?

It's not just incorporating it into my routine, but also remembering to do it. At work, I get so caught up in dealing with stuff, I forget about breathing exercises. Maybe setting reminders on my phone would help?

That's a great idea about setting reminders on your phone, which could definitely help you remember to take a few deep breaths when you're feeling stressed. Can you imagine how having a specific cue, like a reminder on your phone, would make you more likely to follow through with your breathing exercises during those stressful moments?
Yeah, that makes sense. But what if I miss the reminders? Or what if during an argument with my wife, I just can't calm down that easily?

Given that you're worried about missing reminders during an argument with your wife, can you imagine having a trusted friend or family member to call or text when you're feeling overwhelmed, which could provide an immediate distraction and help calm you down?

That's not a bad idea, but would it really work? My business partner is always busy, and my friends aren't always available. What if I end up calling or texting my wife while I'm arguing with her? That'll only make things worse.

You're wondering if having someone to call or text during stressful moments with your wife would really work. Can you think of someone in your life, other than your wife, who knows about your struggles with quitting and might be a good person to reach out to in those situations?

My younger brother, he's always been supportive, even when my wife and I were arguing about money back then. He's not too involved in the business, so he might be less stressed and more level-headed, maybe I could talk to him.

It sounds like your brother could be a great support system for you in moments when you're feeling stressed or overwhelmed, especially during arguments with your wife. Can you imagine how it would feel to have someone like your brother to reach out to, knowing that he's got your back and is there to offer support and encouragement?
Yeah, it would feel good to know I've got someone to talk to, but how can I be sure he'll be there for me? What if he gets busy or forgets?

You're concerned about relying on your brother and not wanting to put too much pressure on him. Can you think about having a simple conversation with him to let him know how much his support would mean to you and how you plan to use him as a resource when you're struggling with cravings?

Actually talking to him about it, that might make things clearer. I can just tell him, look, I'm trying to quit smoking, and I need someone to talk to when I'm stressed. He knows about my smoking habit, maybe it won't be a big deal for him.

You're thinking of having a conversation with your brother to let him know about your quit plan and how you'd like him to be a support system for you. Can you imagine what you would say to him, and how you think he might respond to your request for support?
I think I'd just be honest with him, tell him how much money I'm saving by quitting, and how it'll affect the family if I don't. I'm sure he'll support me, he's always been like that.
It sounds like you're feeling confident and hopeful about this conversation with your brother – I wish you both the best as you move forward together.
Me too, it'll be good to have him in my corner, you know, it's not just about quitting smoking, it's about taking care of my family.

You mentioned it's not just about quitting smoking, it's about taking care of your family. How do you think achieving this goal of quitting smoking will impact your relationship with your wife, particularly since you mentioned she's been a source of stress lately?

If I can quit smoking, it'll show her that I'm serious about changing and taking care of our family's finances. Maybe it'll help her see that I'm committed to our family's well-being, and it'll ease some of the tension between us.

You're mentioning that quitting smoking could help ease the tension between you and your wife, and that's a significant motivator for you. Would you be comfortable having a conversation with your wife about your quit plan, and how you think you could work together to support each other through this process?
I go to the gym 5 days a week but I smoke 7 cigarettes a day. I know it's contradictory.
You're acknowledging the contradiction between your fitness routine and smoking habit. What do you think is driving you to continue smoking despite your efforts at the gym?

I guess it's about relaxing after a tough workout or unwinding late at night, but deep down, it's a little more than that. I feel like if I'm really honest with myself, I'm smoking because my friends at the gym don't know about it, and I'm ashamed to tell them.
